subject: Physics
q: In <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>AM</mi></math> modulation, a signal is modulated on a carrier wave such that maximum and minimum amplitude are found to be <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>6</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi></math> respectively. The modulation index is
question: In <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>AM</mi></math> modulation, a signal is modulated on a carrier wave such that maximum and minimum amplitude are found to be <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>6</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi></math> respectively. The modulation index is
solution:
Given here, Vmax=6 V and Vmin=2 V

Now, modulation index =Vmax-VminVmax+Vmin.×100%

=6-26+2×100%=50%

subject: Physics
q: A modulated signal <math><msub><mrow><mi>C</mi></mrow><mrow><mi>m</mi></mrow></msub><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mi>t</mi></mrow></mfenced></math> has the form <math><msub><mrow><mi>C</mi></mrow><mrow><mi>m</mi></mrow></msub><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mi>t</mi></mrow></mfenced><mo>=</mo><mn>30</mn><mrow><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">sin</mi></mrow><mo>⁡</mo><mrow><mn>300</mn><mi>π</mi><mi>t</mi><mo>+</mo><mn>10</mn><mi> </mi><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mrow><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">cos</mi></mrow><mo>⁡</mo><mrow><mn>200</mn><mi>π</mi><mi>t</mi><mo>-</mo><mrow><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">cos</mi></mrow><mo>⁡</mo><mrow><mn>400</mn><mi>π</mi><mi>t</mi></mrow></mrow></mrow></mrow></mrow></mfenced><mo>.</mo></mrow></mrow></math> The carrier frequency <math><msub><mrow><mi>f</mi></mrow><mrow><msup><mrow><mi>c</mi></mrow></msup></mrow></msub></math> , the modulating frequency (message frequency) <math><msub><mrow><mi>f</mi></mrow><mrow><msup><mrow><mi>m , </mi></mrow></msup></mrow></msub></math>and the modulation index <math><mi>μ</mi></math> are respectively given by :
question: A modulated signal <math><msub><mrow><mi>C</mi></mrow><mrow><mi>m</mi></mrow></msub><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mi>t</mi></mrow></mfenced></math> has the form <math><msub><mrow><mi>C</mi></mrow><mrow><mi>m</mi></mrow></msub><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mi>t</mi></mrow></mfenced><mo>=</mo><mn>30</mn><mrow><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">sin</mi></mrow><mo>⁡</mo><mrow><mn>300</mn><mi>π</mi><mi>t</mi><mo>+</mo><mn>10</mn><mi> </mi><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mrow><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">cos</mi></mrow><mo>⁡</mo><mrow><mn>200</mn><mi>π</mi><mi>t</mi><mo>-</mo><mrow><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">cos</mi></mrow><mo>⁡</mo><mrow><mn>400</mn><mi>π</mi><mi>t</mi></mrow></mrow></mrow></mrow></mrow></mfenced><mo>.</mo></mrow></mrow></math> The carrier frequency <math><msub><mrow><mi>f</mi></mrow><mrow><msup><mrow><mi>c</mi></mrow></msup></mrow></msub></math> , the modulating frequency (message frequency) <math><msub><mrow><mi>f</mi></mrow><mrow><msup><mrow><mi>m , </mi></mrow></msup></mrow></msub></math>and the modulation index <math><mi>μ</mi></math> are respectively given by :
solution:
Compare with

C m (t)= A c sin( w c t)+ μ A c 2 [ cos( w c − w m )t−cos( w c + w m ) ]

μ A c 2 =10

A c =30

μ= 2 3

w c =2π f c =300π ⇒ f c =150Hz

w c − w m =200π

w m =100π

f m =50Hz

subject: Physics
q:
Match List I with List II

  LIST I   LIST II
A AM Broadcast I 88-108 MHz
B FM Broadcast II 540-1600 kHz
C Television III 3.7-4.2 GHz
D Satellite Communication IV 54 MHz- 590 MHz

Choose the correct answer from the options given below:
question:
Match List I with List II

  LIST I   LIST II
A AM Broadcast I 88-108 MHz
B FM Broadcast II 540-1600 kHz
C Television III 3.7-4.2 GHz
D Satellite Communication IV 54 MHz- 590 MHz

Choose the correct answer from the options given below:
solution:
AM broadcasting is radio broadcasting using amplitude modulation (AM) transmissions.

AM Broadcast range → 540–1600 kHz 

FM broadcasting is the method of radio broadcasting that uses frequency modulation (FM).

FM Broadcast range →88 – 108 MHz

Television →54 – 890 MHz

Satellite communication →3.7–4.2 GHz

∴A-II, B-I, C-IV, D-III

subject: Physics
q:
If the height of transmitting and receiving antennas are 80 m each, the maximum line of sight distance will be :

Given : Earth’s radius =6.4×106 m.
question:
If the height of transmitting and receiving antennas are 80 m each, the maximum line of sight distance will be :

Given : Earth’s radius =6.4×106 m.
solution:
Given: R=6.4×106 m

Using the relation between the range of the radio communication and the height of the transmission antenna hT and the receiving antenna hR, the maximum line of sight distance between two antennas, is dmax=2RhT+2RhR

⇒dmax=2×2×6.4×106×80=64000 m=64 km

subject: Physics
q: In amplitude modulation, the message signal Vm(t)=10sin2π×105t volts and carrier signal VCt=20sin2π×107t volts. The modulated signal now contains the message signal with lower side band and upper side band frequency, therefore the bandwidth of modulated signal is α kHz. The value of α is:
question: In amplitude modulation, the message signal Vm(t)=10sin2π×105t volts and carrier signal VCt=20sin2π×107t volts. The modulated signal now contains the message signal with lower side band and upper side band frequency, therefore the bandwidth of modulated signal is α kHz. The value of α is:
solution:
Bandwidth =2×fm

=2×105 Hz=200 kHz

subject: Physics
q: In a communication system operating at wavelength <math><mn>800</mn><mi> </mi><mi>n</mi><mi>m</mi><mo>,</mo></math> only one percent of source frequency is available as signal bandwidth. The number of channels accommodated for transmitting <math><mi>T</mi><mi>V</mi></math> signals of band width <math><mn>6</mn><mi> </mi><mi>M</mi><mi>H</mi><mi>z</mi></math> are (Take velocity of light <math><mi>c</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>3</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mrow><mn>10</mn></mrow><mrow><mn>8</mn></mrow></msup><mi> </mi><mrow><mrow><mi>m</mi></mrow><mo>/</mo><mrow><mi>s</mi></mrow></mrow><mo>,</mo><mi> </mi><mi>h</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>6.6</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mrow><mn>10</mn></mrow><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>34</mn></mrow></msup><mi> </mi><mi>J</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">-</mi><mi mathvariant="normal"><mi>s</mi></mi></math> )
question: In a communication system operating at wavelength <math><mn>800</mn><mi> </mi><mi>n</mi><mi>m</mi><mo>,</mo></math> only one percent of source frequency is available as signal bandwidth. The number of channels accommodated for transmitting <math><mi>T</mi><mi>V</mi></math> signals of band width <math><mn>6</mn><mi> </mi><mi>M</mi><mi>H</mi><mi>z</mi></math> are (Take velocity of light <math><mi>c</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>3</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mrow><mn>10</mn></mrow><mrow><mn>8</mn></mrow></msup><mi> </mi><mrow><mrow><mi>m</mi></mrow><mo>/</mo><mrow><mi>s</mi></mrow></mrow><mo>,</mo><mi> </mi><mi>h</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>6.6</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mrow><mn>10</mn></mrow><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>34</mn></mrow></msup><mi> </mi><mi>J</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">-</mi><mi mathvariant="normal"><mi>s</mi></mi></math> )
solution:
Available signal width =1100×cλ

=1100×3×108800×10-9 Hz=3.75×1012 Hz

Number of available channels =3.75×10126×106

=6.25×105

subject: Physics
q: A modulating signal <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mi>sin</mi><mfenced><mrow><mn>6</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>28</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mn>6</mn></msup></mrow></mfenced><mi>t</mi></math> is added to the carrier signal <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>4</mn><mi>sin</mi><mfenced><mrow><mn>12</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>56</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mn>9</mn></msup></mrow></mfenced><mi>t</mi></math> for amplitude modulation. The combined signal is passed through a non-linear square law device. The output is then passed through a band pass filter. The bandwidth of the output signal of band pass filter will be _____ <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>MHz</mi></math>.
question: A modulating signal <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mi>sin</mi><mfenced><mrow><mn>6</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>28</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mn>6</mn></msup></mrow></mfenced><mi>t</mi></math> is added to the carrier signal <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>4</mn><mi>sin</mi><mfenced><mrow><mn>12</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>56</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mn>9</mn></msup></mrow></mfenced><mi>t</mi></math> for amplitude modulation. The combined signal is passed through a non-linear square law device. The output is then passed through a band pass filter. The bandwidth of the output signal of band pass filter will be _____ <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>MHz</mi></math>.
solution:
Frequencies present in output of square law device

2fc,  fc+fm,  fc,  fc-fm,  2fm,  fm

After passing through band pass filter, frequencies present 

fc+fm,  fc,  fc-fm

Bandwidth of the output signal =2fm

=2ωm2π=6.28×1063.14 =2 MHz

subject: Physics
q: What should be the height of transmitting antenna and the population covered if the television telecast is to cover a radius of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>150</mn><mo> </mo><mi>km</mi><mo>?</mo></math>  The average population density around the tower is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2000</mn><mo> </mo><msup><mi>km</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup></math> and the value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>R</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">e</mi></msub><mo>=</mo><mn>6</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>5</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mn>6</mn></msup><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo>.</mo></math>
question: What should be the height of transmitting antenna and the population covered if the television telecast is to cover a radius of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>150</mn><mo> </mo><mi>km</mi><mo>?</mo></math>  The average population density around the tower is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2000</mn><mo> </mo><msup><mi>km</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup></math> and the value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>R</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">e</mi></msub><mo>=</mo><mn>6</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>5</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mn>6</mn></msup><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo>.</mo></math>
solution:
Radius covered, r=2RHT
150 km=2×6.5×106 mHT
150 km×1032=2×6.5×106HT
HT=1731 m

Population covered =πr22000 km-2
=3.14×(150)2×2000=1413×105

subject: Physics
q:
Match List I with List II

  List I   List II
A Attenuation I Combination of a receiver and transmitter
B Transducer II Process of retrieval of information from the carrier wave at receiver
C Demodulation III Converts one form of energy into another
D Repeater IV Loss of strength of a signal while propagating through a medium

Choose the correct answer from the options given below :
question:
Match List I with List II

  List I   List II
A Attenuation I Combination of a receiver and transmitter
B Transducer II Process of retrieval of information from the carrier wave at receiver
C Demodulation III Converts one form of energy into another
D Repeater IV Loss of strength of a signal while propagating through a medium

Choose the correct answer from the options given below :
solution:
Attenuation is the loss of signal strength in networking cables or connections.

Transducers are used in electronic communications systems to convert signals of various physical forms to electronic signals, and vice versa.

Demodulation is defined as extracting the original information-carrying signal from a modulated carrier wave.

Repeaters are used to extend transmissions so that the signal can cover longer distances or be received on the other side of an obstruction.

subject: Physics
q: If the highest frequency modulating a carrier is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>5</mn><mo> </mo><mi>kHz</mi></math>, then the number of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi><mi>M</mi></math> broadcast stations accommodated in a <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>90</mn><mo> </mo><mi>kHz</mi></math> bandwidth are
question: If the highest frequency modulating a carrier is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>5</mn><mo> </mo><mi>kHz</mi></math>, then the number of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi><mi>M</mi></math> broadcast stations accommodated in a <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>90</mn><mo> </mo><mi>kHz</mi></math> bandwidth are
solution:
B. W. (Bandwidth) =2× maximum frequency at modulating signal

=2×5 kHz

=10 kHz

∴ No of stations accommodate =9010=9

subject: Physics
q:
An amplitude modulated signal is plotted below:
Which one of the following best describes the above signal?
question:
An amplitude modulated signal is plotted below:
Which one of the following best describes the above signal?
solution:
After analysing the graph we may conclude that

(i) Amplitude varies as $8-10 \mathrm{~V}$ or $9 \pm 1$

(ii) Two time period as

$100 \mu$ s (signal wave) $\& 8 \mu$ (carrier wave)

So, equation of AM signal is

$\left[9 \pm 1 \sin \left(\frac{2 \pi t}{T_{1}}\right) \sin \left(\frac{2 \pi t}{T_{2}}\right)\right]$

$=\left[9 \pm \sin \left(2 \pi \times 10^{4} \mathrm{t}\right)\right] \sin \left(2.5 \pi \times 10^{5} \mathrm{t}\right) \mathrm{V}$

subject: Physics
q:
The amplitude of 15sin(1000πt) is modulated by 10sin(4πt) signal. The amplitude modulated signal contains frequency (ies) of

(A) 500 Hz

(B) 2 Hz

(C) 250 Hz

(D) 498 Hz

(E) 502 Hz

Choose the correct answer from the options given below:
question:
The amplitude of 15sin(1000πt) is modulated by 10sin(4πt) signal. The amplitude modulated signal contains frequency (ies) of

(A) 500 Hz

(B) 2 Hz

(C) 250 Hz

(D) 498 Hz

(E) 502 Hz

Choose the correct answer from the options given below:
solution:
The frequency components of amplitude modulated signal are fc-fm, fc, fc+fm

Here, carrier wave frequency is

fc=100π2π=500 Hz

Modulating wave frequency is 

fm=4π2π=2 Hz

So, the frequencies in amplitude modulated signal are =498 Hz, 500 Hz, 502 Hz

subject: Physics
q: A carrier wave with amplitude of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>250</mn><mo> </mo><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi></math> is amplitude modulated by a sinusoidal base band signal of amplitude <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>150</mn><mo> </mo><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi><mo>.</mo></math> The ratio of minimum amplitude to maximum amplitude for the amplitude modulated wave is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>50</mn><mo>:</mo><mo> </mo><mi>x</mi><mo>,</mo></math> then value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi><mo>,</mo></math> is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>_</mi><mi>_</mi><mi>_</mi><mi>_</mi><mi>_</mi><mi>_</mi><mi>_</mi><mi>_</mi><mi>_</mi><mo>.</mo></math>
question: A carrier wave with amplitude of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>250</mn><mo> </mo><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi></math> is amplitude modulated by a sinusoidal base band signal of amplitude <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>150</mn><mo> </mo><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi><mo>.</mo></math> The ratio of minimum amplitude to maximum amplitude for the amplitude modulated wave is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>50</mn><mo>:</mo><mo> </mo><mi>x</mi><mo>,</mo></math> then value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi><mo>,</mo></math> is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>_</mi><mi>_</mi><mi>_</mi><mi>_</mi><mi>_</mi><mi>_</mi><mi>_</mi><mi>_</mi><mi>_</mi><mo>.</mo></math>
solution:
The value of maximum amplitude, Amax=Ac+Am=250+150 V=400 V
, and the value of minimum amplitude,Amin=Ac-Am=250-150 V=100 V
⇒AminAmax=100 V400 V=14=50200⇒x=200 V

subject: Physics
q: In satellite communication, the uplink frequency band used is:
question: In satellite communication, the uplink frequency band used is:
solution: In satellite telecommunication, a downlink is the link from a satellite down to one or more ground stations or receivers, and an uplink is the link from a ground station up to a satellite. Uplink frequency band for satellite communication is 5.925-6.425 GHz.

subject: Physics
q: If a carrier wave $\mathrm{c}(\mathrm{t})=\mathrm{A} \sin \omega_{\mathrm{c}} \mathrm{t}$ is amplitude modulated by a modulator signal $\mathrm{m}(\mathrm{t})=\mathrm{A}$ sin $\omega_{\mathrm{m}} \mathrm{t}$ then the equation of modulated signal $\left[\mathrm{C}_{\mathrm{m}}(\mathrm{t})\right]$ and its modulation index are respectively
question: If a carrier wave $\mathrm{c}(\mathrm{t})=\mathrm{A} \sin \omega_{\mathrm{c}} \mathrm{t}$ is amplitude modulated by a modulator signal $\mathrm{m}(\mathrm{t})=\mathrm{A}$ sin $\omega_{\mathrm{m}} \mathrm{t}$ then the equation of modulated signal $\left[\mathrm{C}_{\mathrm{m}}(\mathrm{t})\right]$ and its modulation index are respectively
solution:
Modulation index
$$
\mathrm{m}_{\mathrm{a}}=\frac{\mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{m}}}{\mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{c}}}=\frac{\mathrm{A}}{\mathrm{A}}=1
$$
Equation of modulated signal $\left[\mathrm{C}_{\mathrm{m}}(\mathrm{t})\right]$
$$
=\mathrm{E}_{(\mathrm{C})}+\mathrm{m}_{\mathrm{a}} \mathrm{E}_{(\mathrm{C})} \sin \omega_{\mathrm{m}}^{\mathrm{t}}
$$
$=\mathrm{A}\left(1+\sin \mathrm{w}_{\mathrm{C}} \mathrm{t}\right) \sin \omega_{\mathrm{m}} \mathrm{t}$
$\left(\right.$ As $\left.\mathrm{E}_{(\mathrm{C})}=\mathrm{A} \sin \omega_{\mathrm{c}} \mathrm{t}\right)$

subject: Physics
q: A transmitting antenna at the top of a tower has a height 32 m and the height of the receiving antenna is 50 m. What is the maximum distance between them for satisfactory communication in line of sight (LOS) mode?
question: A transmitting antenna at the top of a tower has a height 32 m and the height of the receiving antenna is 50 m. What is the maximum distance between them for satisfactory communication in line of sight (LOS) mode?
solution:
Given,

Height of transmitting antenna is, hT=50 m

Height of receiving antenna is, hR=32 m

Thus, maximum distance for satisfactory communication will be,

dM=2RhT+2RhR where R is radius of the Earth,

dM=2RhT+2RhRdM=2×64×105×50+2×64×105×32dM=800010+640010dM=1440010 mdM=45536.7 mdM=45.5 km

subject: Physics
q:
A transmitting antenna at top of a tower has a height of 50 m, and the height of receiving antenna is, 80 m. What is the range of communication for the line of sight (LOS) mode?
[use radius of the earth=6400 km]
question:
A transmitting antenna at top of a tower has a height of 50 m, and the height of receiving antenna is, 80 m. What is the range of communication for the line of sight (LOS) mode?
[use radius of the earth=6400 km]
solution: The formula of range of the antenna is given by the formula,=2Rh1+2Rh2., here R is the radius of the earth then, putting the values, the range =57.28 km.

q: The wavelength of the carrier waves in a modern optical fiber communication network is close to:
question: The wavelength of the carrier waves in a modern optical fiber communication network is close to:
solution: For minimum attenuation, the wavelength of carrier waves is close to <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1500</mn><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">n</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math>

subject: Physics
q: A TV transmitting antenna is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>98</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> high and the receiving antenna is at the ground level. If the radius of the earth is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>6400</mn><mo> </mo><mi>km</mi><mo>,</mo></math> the surface area covered by the transmitting antenna is approximately:
question: A TV transmitting antenna is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>98</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> high and the receiving antenna is at the ground level. If the radius of the earth is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>6400</mn><mo> </mo><mi>km</mi><mo>,</mo></math> the surface area covered by the transmitting antenna is approximately:
solution:
The given data is 

H=98 m

R=6400 km

The line of sight formula for an antenna is 

d=2RH

⇒Area =πd2=π×2×R×h=2×6400×98×10-3×π=3940 km2

subject: Physics
q: If the sum of the heights of transmitting and receiving antennas in the line of sight of communication is fixed at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>160</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo>,</mo></math> then the maximum range of LOS communication is _______ <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>km</mi></math> (Take radius of Earth <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>=</mo><mn>6400</mn><mo> </mo><mi>km</mi></math>)
question: If the sum of the heights of transmitting and receiving antennas in the line of sight of communication is fixed at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>160</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo>,</mo></math> then the maximum range of LOS communication is _______ <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>km</mi></math> (Take radius of Earth <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>=</mo><mn>6400</mn><mo> </mo><mi>km</mi></math>)
solution:
hr+ht=160 (Given)
LOS=2Rhr+ht
LOS=2Rhr+160-hr
dLOSdhr=0=2R12hr-12160-hr=0
hr=ht=80 m
LOS=2R(80+80)=22×6400×103×80=64 km

subject: Physics
q: The height of transmitting antenna is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>180</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> and the height of the receiving antenna is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>245</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math>. The maximum distance between them for satisfactory communication in line of sight will be: (given <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>R</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>6400</mn><mo> </mo><mi>km</mi></math>)
question: The height of transmitting antenna is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>180</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> and the height of the receiving antenna is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>245</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math>. The maximum distance between them for satisfactory communication in line of sight will be: (given <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>R</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>6400</mn><mo> </mo><mi>km</mi></math>)
solution:
The maximum distance for line of sight communication is 

d=2hR+2h'R

The given data is 

R=6400 kmh=180 m

h'=245 m

Thus, the value is

d=2×180×6400×103

+2×245×6400×103

=6×80×102+7×80×102

=104 km

subject: Physics
q: A signal of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>1</mn><mo> </mo><mi>kW</mi></math> is transmitted in a cable. The attenuation of cable is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>-</mo><mn>5</mn><mo> </mo><mi>dB</mi></math> per <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>km</mi></math> and cable length is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>20</mn><mo> </mo><mi>km</mi><mo>.</mo></math> The power received at the receiver is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mi>x</mi></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">W</mi><mo>.</mo></math> The value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math> is _________. [Gain in <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>dB</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>10</mn><mo> </mo><msub><mi>log</mi><mn>10</mn></msub><mfenced><mfrac><msub><mi>P</mi><mi>O</mi></msub><msub><mi>P</mi><mi>i</mi></msub></mfrac></mfenced></math>]
question: A signal of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>1</mn><mo> </mo><mi>kW</mi></math> is transmitted in a cable. The attenuation of cable is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>-</mo><mn>5</mn><mo> </mo><mi>dB</mi></math> per <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>km</mi></math> and cable length is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>20</mn><mo> </mo><mi>km</mi><mo>.</mo></math> The power received at the receiver is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mi>x</mi></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">W</mi><mo>.</mo></math> The value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math> is _________. [Gain in <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>dB</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>10</mn><mo> </mo><msub><mi>log</mi><mn>10</mn></msub><mfenced><mfrac><msub><mi>P</mi><mi>O</mi></msub><msub><mi>P</mi><mi>i</mi></msub></mfrac></mfenced></math>]
solution:
Sound level decreases by 5 dB every km so, sound level decreased in 20 km=100 dB

β2-β1=10 log10I2I1

-100=10 log10I2I1⇒I1I2=1010

I2=10-10I1⇒P2=10-10P1=10-8 W

x=8

subject: Physics
q: A bandwidth of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>6</mn><mo> </mo><mi>MHz</mi></math> is available for <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi><mo>.</mo><mi>M</mi><mo>.</mo></math> transmission. If the maximum audio signal frequency used for modulating the carrier wave is not to exceed <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>6</mn><mo> </mo><mi>kHz</mi><mo>.</mo></math> The number of stations that can be broadcasted within this band simultaneously without interfering with each other will be __________.
question: A bandwidth of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>6</mn><mo> </mo><mi>MHz</mi></math> is available for <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi><mo>.</mo><mi>M</mi><mo>.</mo></math> transmission. If the maximum audio signal frequency used for modulating the carrier wave is not to exceed <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>6</mn><mo> </mo><mi>kHz</mi><mo>.</mo></math> The number of stations that can be broadcasted within this band simultaneously without interfering with each other will be __________.
solution:
Band width =2×n× highest modulating frequency.
6MHz=2×n×6KHz
⇒ n=6×1062×6×103=500

subject: Physics
q: An amplitude-modulated wave is represented by, <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>C</mi><mi>m</mi></msub><mo>(</mo><mi>t</mi><mo>)</mo><mo>=</mo><mn>10</mn><mo>(</mo><mn>1</mn><mo>+</mo><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>2</mn><mi>cos</mi><mn>12560</mn><mi>t</mi><mo>)</mo><mi>sin</mi><mfenced><mrow><mn>111</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mn>4</mn></msup><mi>t</mi></mrow></mfenced><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi></math>. The modulating frequency in <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>kHz</mi></math> will be
question: An amplitude-modulated wave is represented by, <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>C</mi><mi>m</mi></msub><mo>(</mo><mi>t</mi><mo>)</mo><mo>=</mo><mn>10</mn><mo>(</mo><mn>1</mn><mo>+</mo><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>2</mn><mi>cos</mi><mn>12560</mn><mi>t</mi><mo>)</mo><mi>sin</mi><mfenced><mrow><mn>111</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mn>4</mn></msup><mi>t</mi></mrow></mfenced><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi></math>. The modulating frequency in <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>kHz</mi></math> will be
solution:
The function of the amplitude-modulated wave is represented by, Cm=AC1+AmACsin2πfmt, now compare it from the given equation, 
Cm(t)=10(1+0.2cos12560t)sin111×104t V

⇒2πfm=12560 s-1⇒fm=2000 Hz=2 kHz.

subject: Physics
q: A transmitting antenna has a height of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>320</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> and that of receiving antenna is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2000</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo>.</mo></math> The maximum distance between them for satisfactory communication in line of sight mode is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>d</mi></math>. The value of  <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>d</mi></math> is ________ <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>km</mi><mo>.</mo></math>
question: A transmitting antenna has a height of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>320</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> and that of receiving antenna is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2000</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo>.</mo></math> The maximum distance between them for satisfactory communication in line of sight mode is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>d</mi></math>. The value of  <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>d</mi></math> is ________ <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>km</mi><mo>.</mo></math>
solution:
Recall the formula of maximum distance between transmitting antenna and receiving antenna for satisfactory communication in terms of the radius of the earth and height of antennas, 

d=2Rh1+2Rh2⇒d=2×6400×0.32+2×6400×2≈224 km

subject: Physics
q: Only <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mo>%</mo></math> of the optical source frequency is the available channel bandwidth for an optical communicating system operating at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1000</mn><mo> </mo><mi>nm</mi></math>. If an audio signal requires a bandwidth of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>8</mn><mi>kHz</mi></math>, how many channels can be accommodated for transmission
question: Only <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mo>%</mo></math> of the optical source frequency is the available channel bandwidth for an optical communicating system operating at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1000</mn><mo> </mo><mi>nm</mi></math>. If an audio signal requires a bandwidth of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>8</mn><mi>kHz</mi></math>, how many channels can be accommodated for transmission
solution:
Frequency f=cλ=3×1081000×10-9=3×1014 Hz

Band width used for transmission =2100×3×1014 Hz=6×1012 Hz

Number of channels N=Total bandwidth of channel Bandwidth needed per channel =6×10128×103=6008×107=75×107

subject: Physics
q: If a message signal of frequency <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>f</mi><mi>m</mi></msub></math> is amplitude modulated with a carrier signal of frequency <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>f</mi><mi>c</mi></msub></math> and radiated through an antenna, the wavelength of the corresponding signal in air is
question: If a message signal of frequency <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>f</mi><mi>m</mi></msub></math> is amplitude modulated with a carrier signal of frequency <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>f</mi><mi>c</mi></msub></math> and radiated through an antenna, the wavelength of the corresponding signal in air is
solution:
Given frequency of massage signal =fm frequency of carrier signal =fc
the wavelength of the corresponding signal in air is ⇒λ=cfc

subject: Physics
q: In a <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>CE</mi></math> transistor amplifier, the audio signal voltage across the collector resistance of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mo> </mo><mi>kΩ</mi></math> is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi></math>, if the base resistance is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo> </mo><mi>kΩ</mi></math> and the current amplification of the transistor is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>100</mn></math> then the input signal voltage is
question: In a <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>CE</mi></math> transistor amplifier, the audio signal voltage across the collector resistance of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mo> </mo><mi>kΩ</mi></math> is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi></math>, if the base resistance is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo> </mo><mi>kΩ</mi></math> and the current amplification of the transistor is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>100</mn></math> then the input signal voltage is
solution:
Current amplification factor,

β=ΔICΔIB

Collector current,

ΔIC=2V2×103Ω=1×10-3 A

Base current,

ΔIB=VBRB=VB1×103=VB×10-3

Given, β=100

Now, 100=10-3VB×10-3

⇒VB=1100 V=10 mV

subject: Physics
q: A carrier signal <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>C</mi><mo>(</mo><mi>t</mi><mo>)</mo><mo>=</mo><mn>25</mn><mi>sin</mi><mfenced><mrow><mn>2</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>512</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mn>10</mn></msup><mi>t</mi></mrow></mfenced></math> is amplitude modulated by a message signal <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>m</mi><mo>(</mo><mi>t</mi><mo>)</mo><mo>=</mo><mn>5</mn><mi>sin</mi><mfenced><mrow><mn>1</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>57</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mn>8</mn></msup><mi>t</mi></mrow></mfenced></math> and transmitted through an antenna. What will be the bandwidth of the modulated signal ?
question: A carrier signal <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>C</mi><mo>(</mo><mi>t</mi><mo>)</mo><mo>=</mo><mn>25</mn><mi>sin</mi><mfenced><mrow><mn>2</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>512</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mn>10</mn></msup><mi>t</mi></mrow></mfenced></math> is amplitude modulated by a message signal <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>m</mi><mo>(</mo><mi>t</mi><mo>)</mo><mo>=</mo><mn>5</mn><mi>sin</mi><mfenced><mrow><mn>1</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>57</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mn>8</mn></msup><mi>t</mi></mrow></mfenced></math> and transmitted through an antenna. What will be the bandwidth of the modulated signal ?
solution:
Band width =2fm

ωm=1.57×108=2πfm

BW=2fm=1082 Hz=50MHz

subject: Physics
q:
Given below are two statements:

Statement I : A speech signal of 2 kHz is used to modulate a carrier signal of 1 MHz. The bandwidth requirement for the signal is 4 kHz.

Statement II : The side band frequencies are 1002 kHz and 998 kHz. In the light of the above statements, choose the correct answer from the options given below:
question:
Given below are two statements:

Statement I : A speech signal of 2 kHz is used to modulate a carrier signal of 1 MHz. The bandwidth requirement for the signal is 4 kHz.

Statement II : The side band frequencies are 1002 kHz and 998 kHz. In the light of the above statements, choose the correct answer from the options given below:
options: Statement I is false but Statement II is true
Both Statement I and Statement II are true
Statement I is true but Statement II is false
Both Statement I and Statement II are false
solution:
fm=2 kHz

fc=1 MHz=1000 kHz

Band width =2fm=4 kHz

∴ Side frequencies will be

=fc±fm

=1000±2 kHz

=998 kHz & 1002 kHz

So, statement-I & statement-II both are correct.
